title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

This line chart tracks the cumulative number of global parties signing onto twelve distinct multilateral environmental agreements between 1971 and 2015. The vertical axis measures the total count of parties, ranging from 0 to 200, while the horizontal axis spans the years. The lines show a consistent upward trajectory for every agreement, indicating that participation grows over time without declining. The Vienna Convention on Ozone, represented by the dark blue line, begins earliest in 1971 and reaches the highest level, ending near 198 parties by 2015. The UN Framework Convention on Climate Change and the Convention on Biological Diversity follow similar paths, starting in the early 1990s and rising steeply to converge near the top of the chart with roughly 195 parties each. The Kyoto Protocol, shown in maroon, starts later around 1998 and climbs to approximately 192 parties. In the middle of the distribution, the World Heritage Convention and the Cites Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora show steady growth from the 1970s, finishing between 180 and 190 parties. The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety begin near the year 2000 and rise rapidly to reach roughly 170 to 175 parties. At the lower end of the scale, the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals starts in the late 1970s but grows more slowly, ending near 125 parties, which is the lowest value among the visible series in 2015. The data shows that while start dates vary by decades, the dominant pattern is a monotonic increase in the number of signatories for all listed environmental treaties.
